content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**PROJECT TECHNICAL DOCUMENTATION**

**Cold-Start**

**StyleVector**

Personalized Headline Generation for Journalists with Sparse Writing
History

+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| **STUDENT**           | **INSTITUTION**       | **VERSION**           |
|                       |                       |                       |
| **Dharmik Mistry**    | **DA-IICT,            | **v1.0 --- March      |
|                       | Gandhinagar**         | 2026**                |
| 202311039 · M.Tech    |                       |                       |
| ICT (ML)              | MTech Batch 2025 ---  | 6-Week Execution Plan |
|                       | Sem 2                 |                       |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **NOVEL CONTRIBUTION --- VERIFIED ORIGINAL**                          |
|                                                                       |
| Cluster-Centroid Cold-Start Interpolation for StyleVector: No prior   |
| work applies K-means clustering of rich-author style vectors in LLM   |
| activation space to initialize sparse-author vectors via              |
| alpha-weighted interpolation. The specific combination of             |
| StyleVector + cold-start cluster interpolation + Indian journalism    |
| domain is unimplemented in existing literature.                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**1. Problem Statement**

The modern newsroom produces an enormous volume of content, and the
headline is the single most consequential element of any article. A
headline determines whether a reader clicks, shares, or scrolls past.
Beyond click behaviour, headlines carry a journalist\'s identity ---
their tone, vocabulary, sentence rhythm, and editorial stance. An
experienced reader of The Hindu can often identify a Suhasini Haidar
headline by style alone, before seeing the byline.

The problem this project addresses is: how do you automatically generate
a news headline that is both accurate to the article content AND
faithful to a specific journalist\'s writing style?

**1.1 Why Existing Solutions Fail**

Three paradigms exist for personalised text generation, and all three
have significant limitations:

  ----------------------- -------------------------------------------------
  **Supervised            Per-user LoRA adapter requires 17MB of stored
  Fine-Tuning (SFT /      weights per journalist and expensive retraining
  PEFT)**                 whenever new data arrives. Does not scale to
                          hundreds of journalists.

  **Retrieval-Augmented   Retrieves k similar past articles and provides
  Generation (RAG)**      them as context. Suffers from content-style
                          entanglement --- it retrieves topically similar
                          articles, not stylistically similar ones. Fails
                          for sparse authors who have too few articles to
                          retrieve meaningfully.

  **StyleVector (Huang et Extracts a single 4096-dimensional style vector
  al., 2025)**            per user via contrastive activation analysis.
                          Requires 50-287 articles per user to compute a
                          reliable vector. The paper explicitly admits it
                          degrades for sparse users. No solution for the
                          cold-start problem is proposed.
  ----------------------- -------------------------------------------------

**1.2 The Gap This Project Fills**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **The Specific Research Gap**                                         |
|                                                                       |
| The StyleVector paper (arXiv:2503.05213, March 2025) explicitly       |
| states in its limitations section that sparse-user performance        |
| degrades significantly and that cold-start initialisation is left as  |
| future work. No subsequent paper has applied cluster-centroid         |
| interpolation in LLM activation space to resolve this gap             |
| specifically for StyleVector. This project fills that gap with a      |
| measurable, comparable, and deployable contribution.                  |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Beyond the research gap, this project addresses a real social need:
democratising stylistic assistance for journalists. Junior journalists,
freelancers, and regional reporters --- who often have fewer published
articles --- are exactly the users who would benefit most from automated
headline assistance. They are also exactly the users StyleVector fails
for. This project is built for them.

**2. Novel Contribution**

**2.1 Novelty Verification**

A thorough literature search was conducted across arXiv, ACL Anthology,
ACM DL, and Semantic Scholar. Key papers reviewed:

  ---------------------- ------------------------------------------------
  **StyleVector          The base paper this work extends. Proposes
  (2503.05213, 2025)**   contrastive activation steering for personalised
                         generation. Explicitly leaves cold-start as
                         future work.

  **GraSPeR (2602.21219, Addresses sparse personalisation via graph-based
  2026)**                reasoning for review generation. Does NOT use
                         activation steering or style vectors. Different
                         method and domain entirely.

  **PHG-DIF (CIKM        Personalized headline generation via click-noise
  2025)**                denoising. Relies on user click behaviour logs
                         (Microsoft News). Explicitly excludes cold-start
                         users from scope.

  **PENS Benchmark (ACL  Benchmark dataset for personalized headline
  2021)**                generation. Reader-side preference, not
                         journalist-style generation. No sparse-author
                         handling.

  **GTP (TACL 2023)**    Decoupled generation and personalisation for
                         headlines. Uses predefined style control codes.
                         Does not handle the cold-start case.
  ---------------------- ------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Novelty Verdict**                                                   |
|                                                                       |
| The specific combination of: (1) StyleVector\'s contrastive           |
| activation extraction, (2) K-means clustering of rich-author style    |
| vectors in LLM activation space, and (3) alpha-weighted centroid      |
| interpolation for sparse-author cold-start initialisation --- has NOT |
| been implemented or published. The contribution is original.          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**2.2 The Cold-Start Contribution --- Precise Definition**

When a journalist has fewer than 20 published articles, the naive
StyleVector computation produces a noisy, unreliable vector --- too few
(article, headline) pairs exist to average out content-specific noise
and isolate the style signal.

The proposed method --- Cold-Start StyleVector --- addresses this as
follows:

**Step 1 --- Cluster Rich Authors:**

After computing reliable style vectors for all rich authors (50+
articles each), apply K-means clustering to group them into K style
clusters. Each centroid represents a prototypical journalistic writing
style --- for example: \'terse news-wire\', \'analytical long-form\',
\'conversational sports\', \'political commentary\'. Optimal K is
selected by maximising silhouette score over K = 5 to 20.

**Step 2 --- Initialise Sparse Authors:**

For a sparse journalist with only 5-20 articles, compute their raw
(noisy) style vector using the same contrastive activation method.
Normalise it, find the nearest cluster centroid by cosine similarity,
then interpolate:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **v_cold = alpha \* v_centroid + (1 - alpha) \* v_sparse**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

Where alpha is a hyperparameter swept from 0.2 to 0.8 on a validation
set. When alpha = 1.0, the sparse author is assigned entirely to the
nearest cluster (maximum smoothing). When alpha = 0.0, the raw sparse
vector is used unchanged (identical to vanilla StyleVector). The optimal
alpha trades off: how much do we trust the noisy sparse vector vs. the
cluster prior?

**Step 3 --- Use Cold-Start Vector at Inference:**

Replace the sparse author\'s noisy vector with v_cold in the activation
steering step. All inference machinery is identical to the original
StyleVector method. The cold-start module is a pure pre-processing
addition.

**2.3 Why This Works --- Intuition**

Journalistic writing styles are not randomly distributed across
4096-dimensional activation space --- they cluster by genre, register,
and outlet. A political correspondent from any publication likely steers
in a similar direction in activation space compared to a sports
reporter. The cluster centroid encodes this prior knowledge learned from
rich authors. For a new journalist with only 8 articles, their sparse
vector points roughly in the right direction but is noisy. Blending with
the nearest centroid de-noises it using population-level knowledge.

**3. StyleVector --- Paper Explanation**

Before describing the implementation, it is essential to understand
precisely what the StyleVector paper proposes. Everything in this
project builds on top of this foundation.

**3.1 Core Insight**

Large language models maintain internal representations of text in
high-dimensional vector spaces at each transformer layer. These hidden
state vectors encode not just the semantic meaning of text, but also its
stylistic properties --- tone, formality, sentence rhythm, vocabulary
register.

The key insight of StyleVector: if you show the model the same content
written in two different styles (a journalist\'s actual headline vs. a
generic neutral headline), the difference in hidden-state activations at
a specific layer is a direction in activation space that corresponds to
that journalist\'s style. Average this difference over many articles,
and you get a stable style vector.

**3.2 Three-Stage Pipeline**

**Stage A --- Style-Agnostic Response Generation**

For every article in a journalist\'s history, generate a \'generic\'
headline using any general-purpose LLM (Gemini Flash in our
implementation). This headline is content-accurate but stylistically
neutral --- it would be acceptable from any wire service.

This gives us for each journalist u and article i: a pair (y_i_real,
y_i_agnostic) --- the real headline and the generic headline for the
same content.

**Stage B --- Style Vector Extraction via Contrastive Activation
Analysis**

Load LLaMA-2-7B and, for each article-headline pair, extract the hidden
state at a chosen transformer layer L (the last token position):

h_positive = hidden_state_L(article_content + y_i_real)

h_negative = hidden_state_L(article_content + y_i_agnostic)

Difference: d_i = h_positive - h_negative

This difference isolates the style signal from the content signal ---
since both headlines are about the same article, the content information
largely cancels, leaving the style direction.

The journalist\'s style vector is the mean of all differences: v_u =
(1/N) \* sum(d_i for i in 1..N)

This vector is tiny (4096 floats = 16KB) and is computed once. It
captures the journalist\'s style permanently.

**Stage C --- Activation Steering at Inference**

When generating a headline for a new article by journalist u, inject the
style vector v_u into the model\'s hidden states at layer L during the
forward pass:

h_steered = h_original + alpha_steer \* v_u

This nudges the generation in the direction of the journalist\'s style
without modifying any model weights. The effect is that the model
produces text that sounds like that journalist wrote it.

**3.3 Why QLoRA Fine-Tuning Precedes This (Stage 1)**

The base LLaMA-2-7B-chat model is a general-purpose assistant, not a
headline generator. If you extract style vectors from a model that does
not understand the headline generation task well, the contrastive
difference captures a mix of task-confusion noise and style signal.

By first fine-tuning LLaMA-2-7B on a headline generation task using
QLoRA, the model becomes a competent generic headline generator. Now
when you compute the contrastive difference (real headline vs. generic
headline from this tuned model), the content signal is cleaner and the
style signal is more isolated. This is a justified improvement over the
original paper\'s use of the base chat model, and it constitutes the
\'training\' component that satisfies the course rubric.

**4. Dataset Strategy**

**4.1 Primary Dataset --- All The News V2**

All The News V2 is a Kaggle dataset containing approximately 2.7 million
English-language news articles from over 50 US publications including
NYT, Reuters, Guardian, NPR, The Atlantic, and BuzzFeed. It contains
individual author bylines, publication dates, full article bodies, and
headlines.

This dataset is used as the primary source for: (a) QLoRA fine-tuning of
LLaMA-2-7B on headline generation, and (b) rich-author style vector
computation and clustering.

  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------
  **Filtering**      Remove desk accounts, wire services (AP, Reuters),
                     and any entry without a named individual author.
                     Keep only authors with 80+ articles published across
                     3+ months.

  **Scale target**   200 named individual Western journalists, each with
                     80-150 articles. Approximately 20,000-40,000
                     articles for SFT training, 40,000-80,000 for style
                     vector computation.

  **Split**          For each rich author, chronological split: first 70%
                     train, next 15% validation, last 15% test.
","stderr":""}

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**Backend --- FastAPI**

The FastAPI backend serves three endpoints:

-   GET /authors --- returns list of all authors, their article count,
    group (rich/sparse), and cluster assignment.

-   POST /generate --- accepts article text and author name. Loads
    pre-computed style vector for that author (or applies cold-start
    interpolation if sparse). Returns cached pre-generated headline plus
    style metadata.

-   GET /results --- returns the full evaluation table as JSON for
    display in the frontend results page.

The production backend serves only cached pre-computed outputs. No model
runs at request time. Style vectors are 16KB files loaded at startup.
Inference is instant.

**Frontend --- React**

Three screens with simple React components (no Next.js required ---
plain React with Vite is simpler and equally deployable on Vercel):

-   **Demo Screen:** Text area for article input, author dropdown
    showing article count and group badge, side-by-side display of
    Generic vs. Cold-Start personalised headline. Shows which cluster
    the author belongs to.

-   **Results Screen:** Static evaluation table with bar charts
    comparing methods on rich vs. sparse authors. t-SNE visualisation of
    style vector clusters (pre-computed PNG embedded as image).

-   **About Screen:** Project overview, novel contribution description,
    dataset statistics, architecture diagram.

  --------------------- -------------------------------------------------

  deployment**          Free tier. Permanently accessible URL. No GPU
                        required since only cached outputs are served.

  **Frontend            Vercel. Connect GitHub repo. Auto-deploys on push
  deployment**          to main branch. Frontend calls the HF Spaces
                        backend URL.

  **CI/CD**             GitHub Actions: run pytest on every push,
                        auto-deploy to HF Spaces on merge to main.
  --------------------- -------------------------------------------------

**Stage 3B --- Live Research Demo (Presentation Only)**

A separate Colab notebook runs the actual fine-tuned model with real
activation steering:

-   Load fine-tuned LLaMA-2-7B from HuggingFace Hub in 4-bit mode on
    Colab T4.

-   Load pre-computed style vectors for all authors.

-   **On request:** inject the style vector into layer L during
    generation. Return steered headline in approximately 2-3 seconds.

-   Expose the Colab instance via ngrok tunnel to get a public URL.

-   **The frontend has a toggle:** \'Switch to Live Research Mode\'
    which redirects API calls to the ngrok URL.

This setup is run 30 minutes before the presentation and kept alive
during the demo. The permanent Stage 3A deployment serves as fallback if
the Colab session drops.

**6. Full-Stack Architecture**

**6.1 Repository Structure**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| cold-start-stylevector/                                               |
|                                                                       |
| ├── data/                                                             |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── raw/ \# Downloaded datasets, never edited                       |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── processed/ \# Cleaned per-author JSONL files                    |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── splits/ \# Train/val/test splits per author                     |
|                                                                       |
| ├── notebooks/                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── 01_data_exploration.ipynb                                       |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── 02_sft_qlora.ipynb \# Week 3 --- runs on Kaggle                 |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── 03_activation_extract.ipynb \# Week 4 --- runs on Kaggle        |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── 04_cold_start.ipynb \# Week 5                                   |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── 05_evaluation.ipynb \# Week 5                                   |
|                                                                       |
| ├── src/                                                              |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── scraper/ \# TOI/HT scraping scripts                             |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── pipeline/ \# Agnostic gen, activation, cold-start               |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── api/ \# FastAPI backend                                         |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── main.py                                                         |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── inference.py                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| ├── frontend/ \# React + Vite application                             |
|                                                                       |
| ├── vectors/ \# Pre-computed .npy style vectors                       |
|                                                                       |
| ├── outputs/ \# Pre-generated cached headlines                        |
|                                                                       |
| ├── tests/ \# pytest test suite                                       |
|                                                                       |
| ├── docker/                                                           |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── Dockerfile                                                      |
|                                                                       |
| ├── .github/                                                          |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── workflows/                                                      |
|                                                                       |
| │ ├── ci.yml \# Test on every push                                    |
|                                                                       |
| │ └── deploy.yml \# Deploy to HF Spaces on main merge                 |
|                                                                       |
| ├── colab_live_demo.ipynb \# Stage 3B notebook                        |
|                                                                       |
| └── README.md                                                         |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**6.2 Technology Stack**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Layer**              **Technology**     **Rationale**
  ---------------------- ------------------ ------------------------------------
  **Core LLM**           LLaMA-2-7B-chat    Same model as original StyleVector
                                            paper. Results directly comparable.

  **Fine-tuning**        QLoRA              4-bit quantization fits Kaggle T4
                         (bitsandbytes)     16GB. LoRA enables efficient
                                            adaptation without full training.

  **Generic headlines**  Gemini Flash API   Fast, free-tiered. Paper shows
                                            generic model choice minimally
                                            affects style vector quality.

  **Compute**            Kaggle T4 (free)   16GB VRAM. Sufficient for 4-bit
                                            LLaMA-2-7B. Checkpoint upload to HF
                                            Hub prevents data loss.

  **Backend**            FastAPI + Python   Async, Pydantic-native,
                                            auto-generates OpenAPI docs.
                                            Standard for ML inference serving.

  **Frontend**           React + Vite       Simpler than Next.js for this scope.
                                            Deployable on Vercel. You know basic
                                            React.

  **Prod deployment**    HF Spaces + Vercel Both free tier. HF Spaces accepts
                                            Dockerfile on port 7860. Vercel
                                            auto-deploys on git push.

  **Live demo**          Colab T4 + ngrok   Real GPU inference for presentation.
                                            Not a permanent deployment.

  **CI/CD**              GitHub Actions     Free for public repos. Runs tests on
                                            push, deploys on main merge.

  **Containerisation**   Docker             Single Dockerfile for backend.
                                            Ensures reproducible deployment on
                                            HF Spaces.
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**7. CI/CD Pipeline**

The CI/CD pipeline is set up on Day 1 of the project --- not added at
the end. GitHub Actions is used for both continuous integration
(testing) and continuous deployment (auto-deploy to HF Spaces).

**7.1 Continuous Integration --- ci.yml**

Triggers: every push and every pull request to any branch.

Steps: checkout repository, set up Python 3.10, install
requirements.txt, run pytest tests/. If any test fails, the pipeline
blocks and the developer is notified.

Tests cover: API endpoint status codes, style vector loading and shape
validation, cold-start interpolation correctness (alpha=0 returns sparse
vector, alpha=1 returns centroid), agnostic headline generation mock
(API call mocked, output format verified).

**7.2 Continuous Deployment --- deploy.yml**

Triggers: push to main branch only (i.e., after a pull request is
merged).

Steps: checkout with full history, configure git identity, add
HuggingFace Spaces remote using the HF_TOKEN secret, push the repository
subtree to the HF Spaces remote. HuggingFace Spaces detects the
Dockerfile, builds the container, and deploys automatically.

**7.3 Secrets Required**

  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------
  **HF_TOKEN**       HuggingFace write token. Used in deploy.yml to push
                     to HF Spaces. Set in GitHub repo Settings \>
                     Secrets.

  **No other         The backend in production serves only cached
  secrets**          outputs. No API keys are needed at runtime. Gemini
                     API is called only during the offline data pipeline
                     (Week 4) and is not exposed in the deployed backend.
  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------

**7.4 Dockerfile**

The Dockerfile packages only what is needed for the production backend
--- the FastAPI server, pre-computed style vectors, and cached outputs.
It does NOT include PyTorch or the LLaMA model:

Base image: python:3.10-slim. Working directory: /app. Copy
requirements.txt and install. Copy src/api/, vectors/, and outputs/.
Expose port 7860 (HuggingFace Spaces default). CMD: uvicorn api.main:app
\--host 0.0.0.0 \--port 7860.

This produces a small, fast container (under 500MB) that starts
immediately and requires no GPU.

**8. Risk Analysis**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Risk**                 **Likelihood**   **Impact**   **Mitigation**
  ------------------------ ---------------- ------------ ----------------------------
  **Cold-start shows no    **Low-Medium**   **High**     Frame as valid research
  improvement over vanilla                               finding. A rigorous negative
  StyleVector**                                          result is defensible.
                                                         Analyse cluster quality and
                                                         which alpha values
                                                         help/hurt. Document the
                                                         conditions under which
                                                         cold-start fails.

  **Kaggle session crashes **High**         **Medium**   Save in batches of 100.
  during activation                                      Record last processed
  extraction**                                           article_id. Resume from
                                                         checkpoint on next session,
                                                         never restart.

  **Insufficient           **Medium**       **Medium**   Relax sparse threshold to
  individual named                                       5-30 articles. Add The Hindu
  journalists in Indian                                  as a third Indian source if
  dataset after                                          TOI+HT yields under 20
  filtering**                                            authors.

  **LLaMA-2 access delayed **Low**          **High**     Request on Day 1. If delayed
  beyond Week 2**                                        past Week 2, use Mistral-7B
                                                         as fallback (same
                                                         architecture, no approval
                                                         needed). Note the deviation
                                                         in report.

  **Colab session drops    **Medium**       **Medium**   Start Colab 30 min before
  during presentation**                                  presentation. Stage 3A
                                                         permanent deployment serves
                                                         as fallback. Demo is
                                                         designed so either works
                                                         independently.

  **QLoRA training takes   **Medium**       **Low**      Save checkpoint every 500
  longer than one Kaggle                                 steps to HF Hub. Resume
  session**                                              training from checkpoint on
                                                         next session using
                                                         resume_from_checkpoint
                                                         parameter.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**8.1 The Hardest Constraint**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Solo, 6 Weeks --- Zero Buffer**                                     |
|                                                                       |
| This plan is achievable solo in 6 weeks only if every week\'s         |
| deliverables are completed before moving to the next. There is no     |
| buffer. The most likely failure mode is Week 4 (activation            |
| extraction) taking longer than expected and compressing Weeks 5-6.    |
| The mitigation is to start running activation extraction immediately  |
| after the fine-tuned model is saved --- do not wait until Week 4      |
| starts.                                                               |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**9. Evaluation Strategy**

All evaluation follows the original StyleVector paper\'s protocol to
ensure direct comparability. The paper uses LaMP and LongLaMP
benchmarks; this project constructs an equivalent evaluation on its own
dataset.

**9.1 Metrics**

  --------------------- -------------------------------------------------
  **ROUGE-L**           Measures longest common subsequence between
                        generated and reference headline. Captures word
                        overlap at the sequence level. Widely used in
                        summarisation and headline generation.

  **METEOR**            Accounts for synonyms, stemming, and paraphrases.
                        Better than ROUGE for capturing
                        meaning-equivalent expressions. Used in the
                        original paper.

  **BERTScore**         Uses BERT embeddings to compute semantic
                        similarity between generated and reference
                        headline. Captures meaning beyond surface lexical
                        overlap.

  **Style Consistency   Train a linear SVM on rich-author style vectors
  Score (Original)**    as a journalist classifier. Apply to embeddings
                        of generated headlines. Percentage correctly
                        attributed to their target journalist. This
                        metric is not in the original paper and is our
                        additional evaluation contribution.
  --------------------- -------------------------------------------------

**9.2 Four-Method Comparison**

Every result table compares all four methods across two author groups
(rich: 50+ articles, sparse: 5-20 articles):

-   **Baseline 1:** No personalisation --- the fine-tuned LLaMA-2-7B
    generates a headline with no style steering.

-   **Baseline 2:** RAG (BM25) --- retrieve k=3 most similar past
    articles by the author using BM25. Provide as context in the prompt.
    Simple retrieval-based personalisation.

-   **Baseline 3:** Vanilla StyleVector --- standard contrastive
    activation extraction and steering, without cold-start. Shows
    degradation on sparse authors.

-   **Proposed:** Cold-Start StyleVector --- vanilla StyleVector for
    rich authors, cold-start vector for sparse authors. The key result
    is that this method performs well in both groups.

**9.3 Cross-Cultural Evaluation**

As an additional finding, the full evaluation pipeline is also run on
the Indian dataset (TOI + HT journalists). This tests whether style
clusters learned from Western journalism generalise to Indian
journalistic styles. If they do, this significantly strengthens the
contribution. If they do not, this is itself a valid research finding
about the cultural specificity of journalistic style vectors.

**10. Pending Decisions --- Faculty Confirmation Required**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Action Required Before Starting**                                   |
|                                                                       |
| The following two questions must be answered by your faculty before   |
| Week 2 begins. The answers determine whether Stage 1 (QLoRA) is       |
| mandatory or optional, and what constitutes acceptable deployment for |
| the rubric.                                                           |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

  ------------------------- ---------------------------------------------
  **Question 1: Does the    If YES: QLoRA fine-tuning in Week 3 is
  rubric require actual     mandatory and adds significant compute
  model training?**         overhead. If NO: Skip Stage 1. Use base
                            LLaMA-2-7B-chat directly. This frees up 1-1.5
                            weeks of compute time for a solo 6-week
                            project.

  **Question 2: What counts If permanently accessible URL is required:
  as deployed?**            Stage 3A (HF Spaces + Vercel) satisfies this.
                            If live demo during presentation is
                            sufficient: Stage 3B (Colab + ngrok) may
                            suffice. Knowing this changes how much effort
                            goes into Week 6.
  ------------------------- ---------------------------------------------

**10.1 Plan Variants**

If faculty confirms training IS required: execute the plan as written.
Week 3 = QLoRA. Activation extraction in Week 4 uses the fine-tuned
model.

If faculty confirms training is NOT required: skip Week 3. Move All The
News filtering to Week 2. Begin activation extraction in Week 3 using
base LLaMA-2-7B-chat. This gives one additional week of buffer --- use
it on evaluation depth and frontend polish.

Project 2026 · Dharmik Mistry

{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**Backend --- FastAPI**

The FastAPI backend serves three endpoints:

-   GET /authors --- returns list of all authors, their article count,
    group (rich/sparse), and cluster assignment.

-   POST /generate --- accepts article text and author name. Loads
    pre-computed style vector for that author (or applies cold-start
    interpolation if sparse). Returns cached pre-generated headline plus
    style metadata.

-   GET /results --- returns the full evaluation table as JSON for
    display in the frontend results page.

The production backend serves only cached pre-computed outputs. No model
runs at request time. Style vectors are 16KB files loaded at startup.
Inference is instant.

**Frontend --- React**

Three screens with simple React components (no Next.js required ---
plain React with Vite is simpler and equally deployable on Vercel):

-   **Demo Screen:** Text area for article input, author dropdown
    showing article count and group badge, side-by-side display of
    Generic vs. Cold-Start personalised headline. Shows which cluster
    the author belongs to.

-   **Results Screen:** Static evaluation table with bar charts
    comparing methods on rich vs. sparse authors. t-SNE visualisation of
    style vector clusters (pre-computed PNG embedded as image).

-   **About Screen:** Project overview, novel contribution description,
    dataset statistics, architecture diagram.

If faculty confirms training IS required: execute the plan as written.
Week 3 = QLoRA. Activation extraction in Week 4 uses the fine-tuned
model.

If faculty confirms training is NOT required: skip Week 3. Move All The
News filtering to Week 2. Begin activation extraction in Week 3 using
base LLaMA-2-7B-chat. This gives one additional week of buffer --- use
it on evaluation depth and frontend polish.

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**3.3 Why QLoRA Fine-Tuning Precedes This (Stage 1)**

The base LLaMA-2-7B-chat model is a general-purpose assistant, not a
headline generator. If you extract style vectors from a model that does
not understand the headline generation task well, the contrastive
difference captures a mix of task-confusion noise and style signal.

By first fine-tuning LLaMA-2-7B on a headline generation task using
QLoRA, the model becomes a competent generic headline generator. Now
when you compute the contrastive difference (real headline vs. generic
headline from this tuned model), the content signal is cleaner and the
style signal is more isolated. This is a justified improvement over the
original paper\'s use of the base chat model, and it constitutes the
\'training\' component that satisfies the course rubric.

**4. Dataset Strategy**

**4.1 Primary Dataset --- All The News V2**

All The News V2 is a Kaggle dataset containing approximately 2.7 million
English-language news articles from over 50 US publications including
NYT, Reuters, Guardian, NPR, The Atlantic, and BuzzFeed. It contains
individual author bylines, publication dates, full article bodies, and
headlines.

This dataset is used as the primary source for: (a) QLoRA fine-tuning of
LLaMA-2-7B on headline generation, and (b) rich-author style vector
computation and clustering.

  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------
  **Filtering**      Remove desk accounts, wire services (AP, Reuters),
                     and any entry without a named individual author.
                     Keep only authors with 80+ articles published across
                     3+ months.

  **Scale target**   200 named individual Western journalists, each with
                     80-150 articles. Approximately 20,000-40,000
                     articles for SFT training, 40,000-80,000 for style
                     vector computation.

  **Split**          For each rich author, chronological split: first 70%
                     train, next 15% validation, last 15% test.
  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------

**4.2 Secondary Dataset --- Indian Journalism (TOI + HT)**

A custom-scraped dataset from Times of India and Hindustan Times,
constructed from named individual journalists with profile pages on each
publication\'s website.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Current Dataset Status**                                            |
|                                                                       |
| The existing dataset.json contains 1,019 articles from TOI only, all  |
| from Times of India, with 994/1,019 articles having date: Unknown,    |
| and only 2 usable named individual journalists (Sehjal Gupta: 52      |
| articles, Mahima Roy: 44 articles). The remainder are desk accounts.  |
| This dataset must be fully rebuilt with mandatory date extraction.    |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------
  **Target authors** 30-40 named individual journalists. Desk accounts
                     (TOI Business Desk, TOI Sports Desk, HT
                     Correspondent, etc.) are excluded entirely.

  **Target volume**  Minimum 60 articles per retained author. Scrape up
                     to 150 where available.

  **Mandatory        Date is non-negotiable. If the scraper cannot
  field**            extract a date for an article, that article is
                     skipped. No unknown dates.

  **Role in          Secondary validation set. Tests whether the
  project**          cold-start method generalises from Western to Indian
                     journalism styles. This cross-cultural finding is an
                     additional contribution.
  ------------------ ----------------------------------------------------

**4.3 Unified Data Schema**

Every article from both datasets is normalised to this exact schema
before any ML processing:

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| { \"article_id\": \"toi_128455360\",                                  |
|                                                                       |
| \"author\": \"Sehjal Gupta\",                                         |
|                                                                       |
| \"title\": \"Actual headline text\",                                  |
|                                                                       |
| \"content\": \"Full article body text\",                              |
|                                                                       |
| \"date\": \"2025-03-15\",                                             |
|                                                                       |
| \"source\": \"Times of India\",                                       |
|                                                                       |
| \"topic\": \"World\",                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| \"word_count\": 412,                                                  |
|                                                                       |
| \"author_group\": \"rich\",                                           |
|                                                                       |
| \"split\": \"train\" }                                                |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**5. Implementation Plan --- Week by Week**

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Architecture Summary**                                              |
|                                                                       |
| Two-Stage SFT + Steered Inference Pipeline. Stage 1: QLoRA fine-tunes |
| LLaMA-2-7B on headline generation (satisfies course training rubric). |
| Stage 2: StyleVector extraction with cold-start interpolation as the  |
| novel contribution. Stage 3A: Permanent cached deployment on HF       |
| Spaces + Vercel. Stage 3B: Live activation-steering demo via Colab    |
| T4 + ngrok (used during faculty presentation).                        |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

**Week 1 --- Foundation Setup**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Task**         **Detail**
  ---------------- ------------------------------------------------------
  **LLaMA-2        Request access to meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf on
  Access**         HuggingFace on Day 1. Takes 1-3 days. Cannot be
                   deferred --- no access means no activation extraction.

  **API Keys**     Google AI Studio (Gemini Flash, free tier),
                   HuggingFace write token (for model upload), ngrok auth
                   token. All free.

  **GitHub Repo**  Create cold-start-stylevector repo with full folder
                   structure. Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD skeleton with
                   empty test file from Day 1.

  **TOI/HT         Write and run scraper targeting named individual
  Scraper**        journalists only. Mandatory date extraction. Skip any
                   article with null/unknown date. Target: 30-40 authors,
                   60+ articles each.

  **All The News   Download from Kaggle. Run initial filtering script to
  V2**             extract named individual authors with 80+ articles. Do
                   not load full 2.7M articles into memory --- filter by
                   streaming.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Week 2 --- Data Pipeline Completion**

All data engineering must be completed this week. The ML pipeline cannot
start until the data is fully clean, split, and saved. Do not move to
Week 3 until every deliverable below exists on disk.

-   **Run the Indian news scraper. Verify:** every article has a date,
    author is a named individual, content word count exceeds 150 words.

-   **Filter All The News V2. Final author list decided:** rich (80+
    articles) and sparse (5-20 articles). Save per-author article lists.

-   Unify both datasets into the standard schema. Save as per-author
    JSONL files in data/processed/.

-   **Chronological splits:** for each rich author, sort by date, take
    first 70% as train, next 15% as val, last 15% as test. Save to
    data/splits/.

-   **Run data exploration notebook:** author distribution, article
    count histogram, word count statistics, date coverage check.

-   **Deliverable:** data/processed/ and data/splits/ fully populated.
    EDA notebook with plots committed to repo.

**Week 3 --- QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Stage 1)**

This stage satisfies the course requirement for actual model training
--- loss computation, gradient updates, backpropagation. Run entirely on
Kaggle T4 GPU. LLaMA-2 access must be approved before this week begins.

**Training Data**

Use the All The News training split. For each article, construct a
prompt-response pair:

Prompt: \'Generate a concise professional headline for this
article:\\n\\n{article_body\[:1500\]}\\n\\nHeadline:\'

Response: {actual_headline}

Use 20,000-30,000 articles. More will exceed Kaggle session time. Sample
diverse authors and topics.

**Training Configuration**

  ------------------- ----------------------------------------------------
  **Model**           meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, loaded in 4-bit
                      quantisation via bitsandbytes

  **LoRA rank**       16, alpha 32, dropout 0.1, target modules: q_proj,
                      v_proj, k_proj, o_proj

  **Batch size**      4 with gradient accumulation steps 8 (effective
                      batch 32)

  **Learning rate**   2e-4 with cosine decay

  **Epochs**          2 maximum. Stop early if validation loss plateaus.

  **Checkpointing**   Save every 500 steps to Kaggle output directory.
                      Upload each checkpoint to HuggingFace Hub
                      immediately after saving --- Kaggle sessions reset
                      and outputs may be lost.

  **Expected time**   6-10 hours on T4. Run overnight. Monitor via Kaggle
                      notebook.
  ------------------- ----------------------------------------------------

**Deliverable**

Merged LoRA model weights uploaded to your HuggingFace Hub account. The
model generates coherent, grammatically correct headlines of appropriate
length. Validate by running it on 20 unseen test articles and inspecting
output quality manually.

**Week 4 --- Agnostic Headlines + Activation Extraction (Stage 2
Begins)**

**Part A: Style-Agnostic Headline Generation (Days 1-2)**

For every article in your training set, call the Gemini Flash API to
generate a neutral, style-free headline. This is the \'negative\' side
of the contrastive pair.

Run this once. Save results to data/processed/agnostic_headlines.csv
with columns: article_id, agnostic_headline. Estimated 10,000-20,000 API
calls. Gemini Flash free tier (15 requests/minute, 1500/day) means this
takes 10-14 days at free limits --- plan accordingly and consider
batching across days. Alternatively, use the paid tier for a few
dollars.

**Part B: Activation Extraction (Days 3-7)**

Load the fine-tuned LLaMA-2-7B from HuggingFace Hub on Kaggle T4. For
each article in the training set:

-   **Tokenise:** article_content + real_headline. Extract hidden state
    at layer L, last token position. This is h_positive.

-   **Tokenise:** article_content + agnostic_headline. Extract hidden
    state at layer L, last token position. This is h_negative.

-   **Compute difference:** d_i = h_positive - h_negative. Shape:
","stderr":""}
